segments.title: Cutty Sark's rigging reveals clipper complexity
segments.summary: A view aboard Cutty Sark uses her preserved rigging to convey the complexity and scale of a nineteenth-century sailing ship.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke invites viewers to look around the preserved ship and focus on the rigging. Seeing that machinery of sail in place makes the sophistication of fast merchant clippers more immediate than a simple description can.

evidence.note: Aboard Cutty Sark, Dr. Clarke directs attention to the rigging and says it shows how remarkable these ships were.

segments.title: Tea chests standardized the clipper trade
segments.summary: Cutty Sark's tea-trade role opens a compact explanation of standardized cargo boxes before modern intermodal containers.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke presents tea chests as an early globally standardized package for moving goods at mass scale, contrasting them with wool's standardized wrapping. The point connects clipper design to commerce: ships such as Cutty Sark were optimized for speed while carrying cargo packed in repeatable units.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke identifies tea chests as an early standardized transport box and contrasts them with modern intermodal containers and wrapped wool.
He links mass-scale standardized goods transport to the speed-focused role of tea clippers.
